# What a healthy diet looks like

> *Part I asked what nutrients **do**. This chapter asks the question a patient actually
> brings you: **so what should I eat?** The answer is not a nutrient target — it is a
> **pattern**, and one you can build from their own food.*

## A healthy diet at a glance

Part I took food apart — carbohydrate and fat and protein, vitamins and minerals, the gut and
the metabolism that handle them. This chapter puts it back together on a plate. Because the
person in front of you does not eat "45% carbohydrate"; they eat *chicken rice*, or *nasi
padang*, or *thosai and dhal*, and they want to know whether it is any good.

Part II showed how needs *shift* across the life course — folate in pregnancy, calcium and iron in
the teens, more protein in older age. This chapter deliberately steps back to the **shared baseline**
those stages all bend from: the healthy pattern for a general, healthy adult. It is an **archetype** —
the plate's *proportions* hold across life stages while the *portion* and a few *emphases* shift, so you
learn the shape once, then dial it to the person in front of you.

The move that makes this answerable is to stop grading single nutrients and start reading the
whole **dietary pattern**. That is exactly how modern guidelines have gone, and it is why they
turn out to **agree** with each other far more than they differ. We will start from Singapore's
own plate model, hold it up against the major international guidelines to see the shared core,
then meet two named, evidence-rich patterns — **Mediterranean** and **DASH** — with their
landmark trials *and* the caveats those trials carry. We finish where the job actually happens:
translating one target into a real Chinese, Malay or Indian meal.

The goal here is **recognition and translation** — read a plate, compare guidelines, name a
healthy pattern and its evidence, and adapt it to a patient's cuisine. Writing therapeutic meal
plans or prescribing gram targets is a dietitian's work, and knowing where that boundary sits is
part of the skill [@crowley2019].

## Why we have dietary guidelines at all

A dietary guideline is not a study. It is a **consensus synthesis** — an expert body reading the
whole weight of evidence and translating it into advice a population can act on
[@who2020diet; @herforth2019fbdg]. That is its strength (it smooths out the single dramatic
trial) and its limit (it lags the science, and it involves judgement). Guidelines come in two
registers: **numeric thresholds** for populations (limit free sugars, salt, saturated and
*trans* fat) and **plate models** for individuals (proportions you can see). Singapore, like most
countries, publishes both.

## My Healthy Plate — the Singapore anchor

Singapore's patient-facing guide is the Health Promotion Board's **My Healthy Plate**, built on
one memorable proportion — **"quarter, quarter, half"** (QQH) [@hpbHealthyPlate]:

- **½ the plate** — fruit and vegetables
- **¼ the plate** — wholegrains
- **¼ the plate** — meat and other protein

with three companion messages: **choose water** over sugary drinks, **use healthier oils**, and
**stay active**. Notice what it does *not* do: no calorie counting, no grams, no forbidden foods.
It is **proportional and motivational** — a picture, not an arithmetic. That is a deliberate
design choice for first-line public messaging, and it is the anchor for everything else in this
chapter. (For the record: My Healthy Plate dates to 2014, with the page refreshed in 2024. It
is current national guidance, not a new release.)

::: {.callout-note}
## The plate is a *proportion*, not a portion size
QQH tells you the **ratio** of the plate, not how big the plate should be. A rugby player and a
sedentary retiree can both eat "quarter, quarter, half" — the amounts differ, the proportions
do not. This is why the plate travels so well across ages, jobs and cuisines: it fixes the
*shape* of a meal and leaves the *size* to appetite and need.
:::

## Reading an international guideline

Set My Healthy Plate beside the major international guidance and the family resemblance is
obvious. Three worth knowing by structure and intent — not by their numbers, which live in the
*Macronutrients* and *Micronutrients, Hydration & Electrolytes* chapters:

- The **WHO Healthy Diet** is the normative "thresholds" backbone: variety; plenty of fruit and
  vegetables; free sugars under 10% of energy (better under 5%); *trans* fat under 1%; salt
  under 5 g a day [@who2020diet].
- The **American Heart Association's 2021 guidance** reframes heart-healthy eating as a
  **pattern with ten features** — favour vegetables, fruit, whole grains, legumes, healthy
  protein sources and liquid plant oils; minimise added sugar, salt, alcohol and, notably,
  **ultra-processed foods** — and explicitly urges thinking in patterns, not single nutrients
  [@aha2021diet].
- The **US Dietary Guidelines for Americans** are federal policy across the whole life course,
  built around a core of vegetables, fruit, whole grains, varied protein and unsaturated oils,
  with limits on added sugar, saturated fat and sodium [@dga2020].

::: {.callout-warning}
## Guidelines evolve — and are debated
Guidance is a moving, contested consensus, not fixed truth. A new 2025–2030 edition of the US
guidelines was released in January 2026 and drew immediate expert criticism over its handling of
saturated fat and protein. For now, treat the stable 2020–2025 edition as the reference and
watch the debate itself — it is a live case study in *how* dietary evidence gets argued, which is
the skill you build in the *Evidence vs Hype* chapter.
:::

## Guidelines converge — that is the point

Line the guidelines up and the striking finding is how **little** they disagree. A global review
of **90 national food-based dietary guidelines** found nearly universal messages
[@herforth2019fbdg]:

| Shared message | My Healthy Plate | WHO | AHA 2021 | US DGA |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Eat a **variety** of foods | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Plenty of **fruit & vegetables** | ✓ (½) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Prefer **whole grains** | ✓ (¼) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Include **legumes / plant proteins** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Limit **free/added sugar** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Limit **salt** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Prefer **unsaturated** over saturated/*trans* fat | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Limit **ultra-processed foods** | — | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |

The wording and the pictures differ; the substance is the same short list. When a patient asks
"which diet is right?", this table *is* the reassuring answer: the experts broadly agree, and the
agreement is unglamorous — eat mostly plants and whole foods, go easy on sugar, salt and highly
processed products.

## Patterns over nutrients

Why do guidelines increasingly lead with *patterns* rather than *nutrients*? Because we eat food,
not nutrients — and foods act **in combination**. The idea of **food synergy** holds that a whole
dietary pattern can predict health better than any nutrient studied in isolation
[@jacobs2003synergy]. An orange is not a vitamin-C tablet plus fibre plus sugar; a Mediterranean
meal is not the arithmetic sum of its olive oil and its fish. This is *part of why* the AHA now
frames its advice as a pattern and warns against chasing single nutrients [@aha2021diet].

This shift also disarms the perennial "is *[food]* good or bad now?" question. Butter, eggs,
coffee, red meat — asked in isolation, each generates a decade of contradictory headlines. Asked
as *"what pattern is this part of?"*, the noise settles. The reductionist question is the wrong
question; that is a theme the *Evidence vs Hype* chapter returns to in depth.

::: {.callout-note}
## How strong is "patterns beat nutrients"?
The food-synergy argument is a **framework**, not a measured effect size — a way of reading
evidence, well supported in principle but not a number to quote. Pattern-level risk reductions
often look larger than single-nutrient ones, but comparing them fairly is itself a subtle
appraisal problem. Teach the idea; don't oversell the magnitude.
:::

Two named patterns show the idea working. Both are worth knowing — *with* the real limits of
the trials behind them.

## The Mediterranean pattern

**What it is.** A pattern, not a recipe: abundant vegetables, fruit, whole grains and legumes;
**extra-virgin olive oil** as the main fat; nuts and fish regularly; modest dairy and poultry;
little red and processed meat.

**The evidence, with its caveats.** The landmark trial is **PREDIMED**, which randomised
higher-risk Spanish adults to a Mediterranean diet (supplemented with olive oil or nuts) versus a
control diet and reported roughly a **30% relative reduction** in a composite of major
cardiovascular events [@estruch2018predimed]. Two caveats must travel with that headline every
time you cite it:

1. The benefit was **stroke-dominated** — the composite was driven by fewer strokes; heart attack
   and cardiovascular death were **not** individually significantly reduced.
2. The original 2013 paper was **retracted and re-published in 2018** after randomisation
   irregularities came to light; the reanalysis upheld the headline, but the episode is a
   reminder that even landmark trials get corrected.

Observational data broadly agree: an umbrella review of many meta-analyses links higher
Mediterranean adherence to lower cardiovascular mortality, fewer heart attacks and — most
strongly — fewer strokes, plus less type 2 diabetes [@medUmbrella]. That is a genuinely strong
evidence base *and* one built substantially on observational studies. How to weigh a "30%
reduction" — relative versus absolute, composite versus hard endpoint — is exactly the appraisal
work of the *Evidence vs Hype* chapter.

## The DASH pattern

**What it is.** DASH — *Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension* — emphasises fruit, vegetables
and **low-fat dairy**, with whole grains, nuts and legumes, and reduced saturated fat and red
meat.

**The evidence, with its caveats.** In the original controlled-feeding trial, the DASH diet
lowered blood pressure by about **5.5/3.0 mmHg** overall, and by roughly **11.4/5.5 mmHg** in
participants who were already hypertensive [@appel1997dash]. Impressive — but read the design
carefully: participants were **fed** all their food, body weight and **sodium were held constant**
(so the fall came from the *pattern* itself, not from losing weight or cutting salt), the endpoint
was **blood pressure — a surrogate**, not heart attacks or strokes, and the trial ran only about
eight weeks. It is a clean demonstration that a dietary pattern moves a risk factor; it is not,
by itself, proof of long-term event reduction. (Cutting sodium on *top* of DASH lowers blood
pressure further — the sodium story sits in the *Micronutrients, Hydration & Electrolytes* chapter, and the
*therapeutic* use of DASH belongs to the cardiovascular disease chapters later.)

::: {.callout-important}
## The rule for citing any landmark trial
State the benefit **and** its caveat, together, every time: relative versus absolute risk,
surrogate versus hard endpoint, the population studied, the certainty of the evidence. A benefit
quoted without its caveat is how hype begins — and spotting the gap is a skill in its own right
(the *Evidence vs Hype* chapter).
:::

## Popular diets: what to tell patients

Patients don't ask about "dietary patterns" — they ask about **keto**, **intermittent fasting**,
**paleo**, the diet a colleague swears by. You need to engage these credibly: "not recommended" is
not the same as "not worth knowing," and a clinician who can't discuss them loses the room. Read
them with the same **pattern-thinking** lens — is this a coherent, sustainable way of eating, or a
restrictive fad?

One fact cuts across all of them. When diets are tested **head-to-head**, the macronutrient split
barely matters: a healthy low-fat and a healthy low-carb diet produced the **same ~5–6 kg** weight
loss over a year, and — strikingly — neither a person's genotype nor their insulin secretion
predicted which would suit them [@gardner2018dietfits]; trials comparing every mix of fat, protein and
carbohydrate reach the same verdict [@sacks2009pounds]. What predicts success is not the macros but
**the calorie deficit a person can actually sustain** — which comes down to adherence and food
quality. That reframes each fad:

- **Low-carb / ketogenic** — real, fast *early* weight loss, but it attenuates after a few months,
  adherence is hard, LDL cholesterol can rise, and most of the trial evidence is low-certainty
  [@keto2023umbrella]. A tool, not a cure. (Its *therapeutic* use — very-low-calorie diets for
  type-2-diabetes remission — is real but specific, and belongs to the diabetes chapter.)
- **Intermittent fasting / time-restricted eating** — a genuine circadian rationale, but head-to-head
  trials show it is **no better than ordinary calorie restriction**; it helps whoever it helps eat
  less. A reasonable structure for some, not a metabolic reset. (Appraised in depth in the
  *Evidence vs Hype* chapter.)
- **Paleo, "clean eating" and the rest** — usually "work" to the extent they cut ultra-processed food
  and added sugar (good), while excluding whole food groups such as dairy, grains and legumes
  (unnecessary, and a nutrient-gap risk).
- **Plant-based / vegetarian** — the one popular pattern that actually *aligns* with the guidelines'
  plant-predominant message; well-planned it is healthful (mind vitamin B12).

::: {.callout-note}
## The one-line answer
Most diets "work" by the same hidden mechanism: eating **less**, of **better food**, in a way you
can **keep up**. So the best diet for a patient is not the cleverest one — it is the healthy pattern
they will actually sustain.
:::

**How to counsel.** Don't dismiss — that ends the conversation. Ask what the patient is hoping for,
and find the **drivable bit**: if low-carb got them off sugary drinks and refined rice, keep that.
Steer toward whichever sustainable pattern fits their life, anchored to My Healthy Plate. And watch
the one real harm — **restrictive fad diets can trigger disordered eating**, so keep the language
weight-neutral and refer if a red flag shows (a thread the *Assessing Diet & Talking to Patients*
chapter takes further).

## The food environment shapes the choice

Knowing the healthy pattern is only half the problem. People choose food inside an **environment**
— what is sold, priced, labelled and placed within reach — and that environment does much of the
deciding for them. This is why Singapore does not rely on advice alone but engineers the food
environment through national tools:

- **Nutri-Grade** grades packaged and freshly-prepared drinks **A to D** by sugar (and saturated
  fat), with the grade shown front-of-pack and the worst grades facing advertising limits. In a
  randomised trial of the label, shoppers bought drinks with **less sugar** — though the label
  alone did not improve the overall quality of their basket, a reminder that a nudge shifts one
  choice, not a whole diet [@hpbNutriGrade; @nutriGradeRCT2023].
- The **Healthier Choice Symbol** flags packaged products that are better within their category,
  so a shopper can compare at a glance [@hpbHealthierChoice].
- The **Healthier Dining Programme** brings the same idea to hawker stalls and restaurants,
  marking lower-calorie and wholegrain options where people actually eat out [@hpbHealthierDining].

Your job at this stage is to **understand this framing** — that policy and environment shape what
lands on the plate — and to point patients toward the tools. Actually *reading a nutrition label
or a Nutri-Grade grade with a patient* is a clinical skill in its own right, taught in the
*Assessing Diet & Talking to Patients* chapter.

## From guideline to a real plate

Here is where it all becomes useful. My Healthy Plate is one target; a Singaporean eats it three
ways. The point is **not** to hand a Chinese, Malay or Indian family a "Mediterranean diet" —
that is a pattern to *adapt*, not a menu to *impose*. It is to rebuild their **own** cuisine to
the quarter-quarter-half shape.

Build a balanced dinner in each of the three cuisines below. Each household starts on its habitual
plate — the one that walks into a real clinic — and you swap the slots until all four are on
target. Watch how the *same* target is reached with entirely different dishes.

<div data-island="healthy-plate" data-src="/diagrams/healthy-plate.yml"></div>

::: {.callout-note}
## 🔬 Food science: why the *pattern* does the work
No single swap on that plate is magic. Brown rice over white nudges the glucose response; oily
fish adds omega-3s; the vegetable half brings fibre, potassium and polyphenols. It is the **whole
assembly** — more plants and fibre, less refined starch, better fat, water instead of sugar —
that tracks with better health. That is food synergy made concrete: you are not dosing a nutrient,
you are shifting a pattern [@jacobs2003synergy].
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍜 Eating out
Hit the plate with hawker choices: ask for **more vegetables** (a side of stir-fried greens, extra
*ulam*, a vegetable *subji*) and a **smaller rice scoop**; choose **steamed or grilled over
fried** (sliced-fish soup, *ikan bakar*, *thosai* with dhal); recognise **dhal, *tau kwa* and
legumes** as a full protein quarter; and default to **water or an unsweetened drink** over a
Nutri-Grade C/D one [@hpbHealthyPlate].
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍳 Healthy kitchen
At home the plate is easier to hit: cook **half the pot as vegetables**, switch to **brown or
mixed rice** or a **wholemeal chapati**, build meals around **fish, tofu, eggs and legumes**, keep
the cooking oil **unsaturated and light**, and go easy on coconut gravy and ghee. Small, repeated
shifts in the everyday meal beat any occasional "superfood".
:::

## The Singapore picture

Why press so hard on wholegrains and drinks? Because that is where Singapore's real gap sits. The
**National Nutrition Survey 2022** found that only about **4% of the staples** Singaporeans eat
are wholegrain — against the quarter-plate the guideline asks for — and that roughly **nine in ten
residents exceed the recommended sodium** intake [@mohNNS2022]. So the two highest-yield moves are
already on the plate you just built: **shift refined rice and noodles toward wholegrains**, and
**cut the free sugar and salt** — the brown-rice swap and the water-over-sugary-drink swap are not
small print, they are the two biggest levers in the local diet.

Culturally, this is a feature, not a compromise. Singapore's three major cuisines are a built-in
laboratory for "same guideline, three menus": the plate survives contact with the family table,
the festival spread and the hawker centre precisely because it adapts rather than replaces. A diet
a patient will actually keep beats a "better" one they abandon by Friday.

## So — is there one best diet?

Put it together and the anxious question dissolves. There is **no single best diet** — there is a
**short list of shared principles** that nearly every national guideline converges on, expressed as
a plate you can see, supported by patterns like Mediterranean and DASH whose trials you cite *with
their caveats*. The best diet for a patient is the **healthy pattern they will actually eat**, built
from their own cuisine, in an environment nudged to make the better choice the easy one. Your role
now is to **recognise a good pattern, compare it to a guideline, and translate it to real food** —
and to hand over to a dietitian when a plan turns therapeutic or restrictive [@crowley2019].

## Check your understanding

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/dietary-guidelines-patterns.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: three households, one plate

A community-health volunteer asks you to help three families eat "more healthily" using My Healthy
Plate — a Chinese, a Malay and an Indian household, each with their own habitual dinner and their
own worries. Adapt the plate to each family's food, tackle the two real local gaps, and handle the
relative who is sure that "PREDIMED proves the Mediterranean diet prevents heart attacks." Your
choices branch the case.

::: {.callout-tip}
Progress is saved locally — replay to explore the other households.
:::

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/three-households-one-plate.case.yml"></div>
